Issue No.22 	• May 2013
Does President Oba.Dla
Really Got E111ail Ga111e?
What up Email Player...
Late last year there was a bit of a tizzy in the Internet marketing world about
emails and how the Obama campaign used them to generate a ho' bunch of the green
stuff. I must have gotten the following news story link sent to me by at least a half dozen
people or so. And basically, it was all about the so-called "science" behind Obama's
fundraising emails -- including the subject lines and psychology employed. Before reading
further, make sure you check the article out (don't worry, it's short) here:
www.Em.ailPlayers.coJD/ oba~na
And man were people excited! Even on the forums I saw all the copywriting
fanboyz going nutzo over this and it didn't take long for the goo-roo fanboyz to start
taking Obama's email subject lines word-for-word since "that's what's working!!!" No
doubt there are some awesome lessons to be learned from the article. And it's a great
teaching for any serious email marketing student to study.
So let us take the article's points one-by-one, and see what's going on:

"Most of the $690 million Obam.a raised online
came from. fu.ndraising e-m.ails."
You know, I sometimes catch flack for saying how worthless social media is for
direct sales. And, how the social media kool-aid drinkers are basically the equivalent of
the children at the kids table shouting and screaming, desperate for attention. And, this is
especially true of the echo chambers who hang out on FaceBook, etc. But, I also get flack
from direct mailers, too. They tell me email is nothing compared to direct mail. And that
there is simply no comparison. But you know what? I see what they're saying and don't
necessarily argue the point. But it's hard to beat $690 million buckaroos (most of which
was acquired by good old, "retro" email). Maybe if you have some scratch to spend and
test with it's great to do direct mail. But for the regular guy who's broke and just wants to
get a toe hold in his business to get started? Are you really going to spend a few thousand
dollars testing a direct mail piece, then doing a roll out or whatever and keeping track of
all the moving parts that go with it? Probably not. But email? All you need is a list and
auto-responder account. The "moving parts" are pushing a button.
Incidentally, what I find most amusing is all the social media kool-aid drinkers who
did NOTHING but talk about how great social media was for Obama, etc when the
Obama marketing team readily admits email was their biggest online fundraising source.
Anyway, onto the next part of the article about email subject lines. Here are the

The most popular Oba.Dla
Michelle time
Deadline: Join Michelle and me
Thankful every day
The one thing the polls got right ...
Okay, so those were the most profitable subject lines in order. And again, no sooner
was this published than I was hearing a flurry of reports about email marketers swiping
these subjects lines (word-for-word), bombarding the Internet with them or variations of
them. Yikes. I don't know what's scarier:
1. People blindly swiping these, when they are built around
Obama's rockstar reputation and positioning amongst his list
or...
2. Voters donating money to an email that says "Michelle
time"
Don't get me wrong. There ARE a couple good subject lines above. Specifically:
"The one thing the polls got right" "Some scary numbers" and "Would love to meet you"
Those are universally applicable. In fact, I may even play on those myself some time. But
the rest? They are built around someone with a rockstar following. And unless you have
that kind of passionate group of people reading your stuff (who will hang on your every
word and blindly buy whatever it is you're selling without question), you probably aren't
going to get away with saying to do something for your wife, yo?
Think hard about that.
Positioning is everything in email.
This is why the "From" line is more important to ye olde bottom line than any
subject line you can cook up. Alright, on to the next part of the article:

"The appeals were the product of rigorous

Another thing I get flack for is saying you can't "scientifically" test emails. It's
impossible due to how REAL scientific experiments are conducted. But, the way they did
it is about as close as you can get. And it helps to have a team of analysts, 20 staff writers
and a list of tens of millions of people to do it with. Point being, if you don't have a huge
list and lots of time and resources to hire this stuff out, don't blindly trust email test
results ...

Another unexpected hit: profanity. Dropping in IDild

There is a TON of wisdom in this paragraph and I suggest reading it again,
several times until you've got it practically memorized. Specifically, the part about the
novelty wearing off. This is EXACTLY why being a slave to test results in email will get
you killed. Something that works today may very well NOT work tomorrow, while
something that flopped today could kill it for you tomorrow. You just never know. That's
why you gotta be out there every day swinging away and not trusting or relying on anyone
elses subject lines, ideas or tests. Every email and market and publisher-reader
relationship is different.
Finally:

How To Profit From. Dead People
I KNOW this sounds a bit morbid on the surface. But really, it can:
( 1) make you a lot of scratch and ...
(2) show respect for the dead.
I call it "the eulogy email."
And this is something you write when a guru or teacher or someone of great
influence in your market joins the choir invisible and you write an email about it. You
basically can tell your readers what you learned from the person. The result is a great
email people love to read that lets you pay your final respects. And, a nice "side effect" of
this is, all the prestige and positioning of the person you write about sort of rubs off on
you -- giving your a nice dose of credibility in the process. Here's how I did it last year
when the great marketer and businessman jim Straw passed away:
-8-

See how easy that is? It's also kind of fun and therapeutic, too, as it helps you
better learn and internalize the stuff you learned from whoever you are giving a eulogy
to. Also, note how I segue smoothly (in context) from the eulogy to the pitch? That way
the pitch doesn't call a bunch of attention to itself. In fact, I suggest ALWAYS doing this
in good taste. Don't come off as like you're trying to purposely exploit the death for your
own gains. Make sure you do this out of respect for the departed first (That's the whole
point of the email, to pay your last respects). Yes, you are going to pitch your stuff (softly,
like I did, preferably in the P.S.). But, it's first and foremost gotta be about the person who
died, and not you. If you don't play this the right way, and instead come off as some kind
of weird opportunist, this can easily blow back in your face (and rightfully so). Not only
will people not want to buy from you, but will likely get a bad taste in their mouths while
reading your email.
Anyway, moral of the story?
Know and respect your market and the deceased if you do this kind of email.

How To Make Out Like A Bandit
With ''Q&A'' Ernails
A lot of y'all who have sent me your monthly free critique email are missing out on
a seriously easy way to make sales by not using more "Q&P:.' emails. Whenever someone
asks you a question, instead of answering it for free (I don't answer peoples' questions
unless they are "Email Players" subscribers, as that's a perk reserved ONLY for y'all)
answer it publicly, and turn it into a reason to buy your righteous product. The best ones
are when someone asks a question that talks about the pains and hot buttons of the
market. When you see one, use it! Here is one I did in the weight loss market that kicked
so much gluteus assimus, I even converted it to a solo email ad. You can model this pretty
easily, just insert your market's big question (walking through their most urgent hot
buttons) and answer it. (Note: If you're in the weight loss market, don't use this word for
word).
Subject Line: 	"I gave up and started eating like a pig again!"
Hey, 	I 	sometimes get emails like this:
"_, 	I 	feel awful! 	I was on a diet and was losing a little bit of
weight, 	but it was happening too slowly and I was tired of always
being hungry and having to watch every morsel I 	put in my body. 	I
felt like a prisoner! And was 	just so depressed and decided to
throw in the towel and start eating whatever I wanted to. 	I 	gained
a lot of my weight back and just now decided I 	do not want to go
down this path. 	Is there anything I 	can do to get back on the
saddle quickly and lose the regained weight faster? 	I have a second
wind now and really want to get this weight off!"
I 	understand!
And believe me when I 	say you are NOT alone!
We all go through that.
I cannot think of anyone who lost a lot of weight who did not quit
and come back and then eventually conquer and YOU will tool
Here's what I 	suggest:
Throw out whatever diet you were on.

Sometimes I think I'm like the lone wolf in the wilderness when I say
unsubscribes/ opt outs are GOOD... and are often an indication that you're both doing
something right, and making more money than you would otherwise if you were worried
about unsubscribes instead of sales (i.e. playing to not lose instead of playing to win).

following in an online mastermind I lurk at that illustrates my point point perfectly. And,
if nothing else, should get you HOT about mailing often and eagerly chasing after
unsubscribes instead of worrying about them.

Ann Coulter's
''Easy Email'' Writing Secret
Sometimes people will ask me about how to write in a conversational and natural
way. It doesn't come easy to a lot of people. And they wisely know if they can get over this
hump, their sales will start shooting up from their emails as people love reading
personality driven emails. Usually what I tell people who ask about this is, to just pretend
you're writing to your best friend. Say what you'd say, don't edit yourself. When you're
done, then go back and edit if you want. But on that first draft go crazy, and be yourself.
Don't worry about what anyone will think, spelling, grammar or anything. Just write and
have FUN. Another technique for doing this is from Ann Coulter (very good at
controversial writing). And love her or hate her (very few "luke warm" reactions to Ann
Coulter), she once dropped an incredible "copywriting" tactic in a radio interview you
can start using immediately if you want in your emails. In fact, when I first heard this tip,
I started doing it and my writing immediately got easier and improved dramatically.

-- 15 of 44 --

THE 	EMAIL PLAYERS 	NEWSLETTER
Anyway, she said when she sits down to write a newspaper column or book chapter --
especially if it involves a complex topic -- she simply pretends she's writing an email to
one of her friends. Not to an editor or critic, a FRIEND. She says this forces her to keep
even the most entangling legal and policy details she writes about simple and easy to
digest for the average person. This is extremely useful copywriting advice. Especially since
the ability to make the complex simple is one of THE most important email skills you can
have. Plus, it takes all the pressure off, stops you from censoring yourself and lets your
unique personality shine through. Besides that, give yourself complete freedom to be
yourself and remember you don't HAVE to send an email you write, so there's no
pressure.
In fact, it all com.es down to taking pressure off.
Forget the whole "gun to the head" mindset copywriters do for deadlines.
Do the opposite with emails (especially when writing DAILY emails). Take ALL
pressure off yourself and emails automatically get much easier.
Word up,
Ben Settle

The power of a good story can do all the heavy lifting for you.

The movie "Top Gun":
When it hit movie theaters Navy recruitment went through the roof. Frankly, it did such a good job of
"selling" people on joining the Navy, the government set up recruitment booths in theaters. Plus, the sun glasses
Tom Cruise wore in the movie also went up 30%.
And yet. nowhere in the moyie does it make a pitch for the Navy.
Or, for those sunglasses, either.

Crude 	Irish 	Swashbuckler 	by

Morlcetlng Strategist.
Dan iel Le vis
Jimmy Ray Carl could sell jus t about anything to
anyone ...
As a greenhorn salesman in the territory. my first
assignment was to accompany him on two or three sa les
calls a day- and observe. Then we'd spend an hour or
so role-playing back at the office.
I learned how to ask questions. And tell stories.
He had a story for countering every objection ... fascinating stories about
how a given product came about... heartwarming stories that made
people like him and trust him. And when he told these stories to custome rs
I watched the purchase reluctance literally disappearing from th eir faces.
Jimmy Ray Carl influenced people so effortlessly and easily that getting
them to sign on the dotted line was like taking candy fr o m a baby.
At firs t I resisted modeling his approach ...
It was hard enough remembering product specs and features and
benefits and competi ti ve advantages. Was it really necessary
complicating things further with people and places and events?
One day we were out for a few beers at a li ttle Italian bistro on Queen 's
Quay (Lake Ontario) . and I asked him, "What's with the stories? Why don't
you just get to the point and tell the customer the facts? "
He looked at me with that mischievous Irish glint of his , and said:
.. Storytelling is a Lot Like Making Love. All Of Us Know
How to Do lt. But Few Of Us Will Ever Be Porn Stars . As
A Persuader, THAT Is Your Greatest Falling ."

He went on, "When you tell stories, people listen. Haven't you noticed?"
I thought about the morning's sales calls, and he was right.
"It's because you're making it easy for them to get the picture" he
continued. 11
They don't have to think too hard about what you're saying.
They just have to watch the little mental movie running in their heads. You
like it when customers listen to you, don't you?"
"Well yeah", I said.
"What's more", he added, "they understand you a whole lot better.
"See, whether you tell stories or not, people are trying to visualize what
you're saying to them. Why risk having your buyers conjuring up the wrong
pictures? Show them what you want them to see with your words.
"And here's another thing. You ever heard the expression, 'seeing is
believing'?"
I'm sure my head had been bobbing up and down the whole time.
Jimmy took a moment to flirt with the waitress and order another pitcher
of beer before he continued ...
"See, when you got this mental movie thing going on, it's like you can say
things to people without actually coming out and saying them.
"Have you noticed how when you make a product claim, the customer is
fighting you? You've noticed that right?"
"Sure. He's thinking ... of course you would say that. You're here to sell me
this thing."
"Yeah. But if you make him see himself interacting with the claim in his
imagination, there's no resistance. He thinks he figured the whole thing
out by himself. And he accepts it, because he owns that idea.
"And here's the really beautiful part Danny boy ...
"See this solid gold Movado watch ... that amazing Jag in the parking
lot ... the block of rental properties I own on the Danforth. Paid for with
